children.name: Proteus anguinus
children.description: Blind, pale, neotenic cave dweller of Dinaric karst in the Balkans; navigates by electroreception and chemoreception; can survive 10 years without food; documented individuals over 100 years old; IUCN Vulnerable; the 'human fish' of Slovenian cave legend.

children.name: Necturus maculosus
children.description: Permanently aquatic; retains large bushy external gills throughout adult life; bottom-dweller of eastern lakes and rivers; named from the false belief it could bark like a dog.

children.name: Necturus lewisi
children.description: A narrow endemic restricted to the Neuse and Tar River drainages of the North Carolina Piedmont, making it one of the most geographically limited salamanders in North America. Like all waterdogs it is permanently aquatic and neotenic, retaining large, feathery external gills throughout its life. It inhabits larger rivers and their tributaries, sheltering under rocks, logs and leaf packs in moderate to fast current over sandy or gravelly substrates. Nocturnal and secretive, it forages for crayfish, aquatic insects and small fish. Threatened by declining water quality from agricultural runoff and urban development within its small watershed; IUCN Near Threatened.

children.name: Necturus alabamensis
children.description: An IUCN Endangered species confined to the upper Black Warrior River system in Alabama — one of the most imperilled salamanders in North America. It inhabits fast-flowing, clear streams with rocky substrates in the Ridge and Valley and Cumberland Plateau regions, conditions that have become increasingly rare as mining, agriculture and urbanisation have degraded water quality and smothered stream beds with silt. Survey data suggest severe population declines across much of its historical range; it persists mainly in protected or less-disturbed headwater tributaries. Breeding biology is poorly known; like other waterdogs it likely nests under flat rocks in spring and the female guards her eggs.

children.name: Necturus louisianensis
children.description: Inhabits the Red River drainage of Louisiana, Arkansas, Oklahoma and Texas — a low-gradient, turbid river system quite different from the clear upland streams favoured by most of its relatives. Long treated as a subspecies of the common mudpuppy, molecular and morphological work supports recognition as a distinct species. It tolerates higher silt loads and warmer, more oxygen-depleted waters than other Necturus, sheltering under submerged wood and debris along muddy river banks. Like its relatives it is fully aquatic, neotenic and nocturnal, feeding on crayfish, aquatic invertebrates and small fish.
